title: We Actually Fixed the Oscars | The Big Picture (Ep. 79)
format: The Academy announces a new 'popular film' Oscar category, and the crew spends the hour redesigning the entire ceremony from scratch.

segments.heading: The New 'Popular Film' Category
segments.note: the Academy's proposal, and whether Black Panther gets ghettoized

films.title: Black Panther
films.note: The movie the whole category seems to be invented around: over $700 million and, everyone agrees, actually great.

films.title: Avatar
films.note: Nominated the year the Academy expanded Best Picture to fix a snub like this one; still lost.

films.title: The Hurt Locker
films.note: Beat Avatar for Best Picture: the small movie over the giant one.

films.title: The Dark Knight
films.note: Its Best Picture snub is why the field expanded to ten nominees in the first place.

films.title: Titanic
films.note: Proof a giant hit can also just win Best Picture outright.

films.title: Moonlight
films.note: The small-movie winner the Oscars get accused of favoring over hits.

films.title: Get Out
films.note: Nominated for Best Picture (inconceivable a decade earlier) and should have won it, per Sean.

films.title: 12 Years a Slave
films.note: The worry: a popular-film category could shrink Best Picture down to just movies like this.

films.title: Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri
films.note: Won by 'sneaking in' last year, unlike a movie that's too successful.

films.title: Dunkirk
films.note: Checked every Best Picture box and still never had a real shot.

films.title: Pretty Woman
films.note: Not a Best Picture nominee: the test case for whether the Oscars owe the year's biggest hit a nod.

films.title: Aquaman
films.note: Bill's December tentpole of choice, invoked as exactly what this category is for.

segments.heading: Fixing the Actual Telecast
segments.note: pacing, montages, and the case for a 150-minute rule

films.title: The Sound of Music
films.note: The interpretive-dance 50th-anniversary tribute they'd cut without hesitation.

films.title: Phantom Thread
films.note: Mark Bridges' costume-design win came with a genuinely fun jet-ski bit, a rare telecast moment they'd keep.

segments.heading: The Grammification of the Oscars
segments.note: five-night ceremonies, public vote totals, and a Best Comedy category that should already exist

films.title: Borat: Cultural Learnings of America for Make Benefit Glorious Nation of Kazakhstan
films.note: Nominated for Adapted Screenplay: proof the Academy isn't as stuffy as it acts.

films.title: Hustle & Flow
films.note: Three 6 Mafia's Best Original Song win. The Oscars have gone populist before.

films.title: Coco
films.note: Cordoned off into Best Animated Feature and never seriously in the Best Picture conversation, despite being better than the field.

films.title: Darkest Hour
films.note: The Best Picture nominee Coco was 'significantly better than,' per Sean and Amanda.

films.title: The Tourist
films.note: The Golden Globes' comedy/musical category once nominated this Depp-Jolie flop, Exhibit A for why 'popular' categories go sideways.

segments.heading: The Alternate History: What Would Have Won
segments.note: running the popular-film award back through Oscar history, year by year

films.title: Jaws
films.note: The easy pick to lead off the alternate history.

films.title: Star Wars
films.note: Lost Best Picture to Annie Hall in real life; wins this one outright.

films.title: Star Wars: The Last Jedi
films.note: Sean's pick: genuinely strange it missed a Best Picture nomination.

films.title: Logan
films.note: The rare superhero movie the Academy did nominate, for Adapted Screenplay.

films.title: Wonder Woman
films.note: Amanda's self-declared 'one bad take' of 2017: she had it at No. 3 for the year and still defends loving it.

films.title: Deadpool
films.note: Sean's half-serious 2016 pitch: nominate it for Best Picture to reflect what movies actually were that year.

films.title: La La Land
films.note: The Oscars didn't need the stunt that year. Moonlight beating it live was drama enough.

films.title: Erin Brockovich
films.note: Julia Roberts' Best Actress win: a career-achievement award, not her best performance, per Bill.

films.title: You Can Count on Me
films.note: Laura Linney's performance that year, per Bill, still holds up better than Roberts'.

films.title: The Contender
films.note: Joan Allen, Bill's pick for the year's actual best actress performance.

films.title: Gravity
films.note: Sean's nominee for the award's most obvious use case.

films.title: The Shape of Water
films.note: Sean wasn't for it even on Oscar night; likes it even less now.

films.title: Skyfall
films.note: Bill's personal pick: peak Bond craft, and a movie he owns on Amazon because it's otherwise hard to watch.

films.title: Casino Royale
films.note: Critic Adam Naiman's pick: 'a sin' it wasn't nominated, and would win this award every time, per Bill.

films.title: There's Something About Mary
films.note: One of the funniest, most successful comedies of its year, nowhere near the Oscars.

films.title: Misery
films.note: Kathy Bates' Best Actress win: exactly the popcorn-movie Oscar this category is meant to formalize.

films.title: Call Me by Your Name
films.note: Timothée Chalamet's Best Actor nomination lost to Gary Oldman, the case for a separate Breakthrough Performance award.

films.title: The Devil Wears Prada
films.note: Meryl Streep got nominated, but Bill argues the film itself belonged in Best Picture.

films.title: The Departed
films.note: Would have cleaned up in the popcorn category too, but its real Best Picture win was a makeup call for Scorsese's long losing streak.

films.title: Fast Five
films.note: Bill's case for the 'greatest action movie of all time.'

films.title: Terminator 2: Judgment Day
films.note: Sean's rebuttal to the Fast Five claim.

Borat: Cultural Learnings of America for.title: Borat: Cultural Learnings of America for Make Benefit Glorious Nation of Kazakhstan
